rawText:
One of the most common types of advice we give at Y Combinator is to do things that don't scale. A lot of would-be founders believe that startups either take off or don't. You build something, make it available, and if you've made a better mousetrap, people beat a path to your door as promised. Or they don't, in which case the market must not exist.

Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off. There may be a handful that just grew by themselves, but usually it takes some sort of push to get them going. A good metaphor would be the cranks that car engines had before they got electric starters. Once the engine was going, it would keep going, but there was a separate and laborious process to get it going.

The most common unscalable thing founders have to do at the start is to recruit users manually. Nearly all startups have to. You can't wait for users to come to you. You have to go out and get them.

Stripe is one of the most successful startups we've funded, and the problem they solved was an urgent one. If anyone could have sat back and waited for users, it was Stripe. But in fact they're famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition.

Startups building things for other startups have a big pool of potential users in the other companies we've funded, and none took better advantage of it than Stripe. At YC we use the term "Collison installation" for the technique they invented. More diffident founders ask "Will you try our beta?" and if the answer is yes, they say "Great, we'll send you a link." But the Collison brothers weren't going to wait. When anyone agreed to try Stripe they'd say "Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot.

There are two reasons founders resist going out and recruiting users individually. One is a combination of shyness and laziness. They'd rather sit at home writing code than go out and talk to a bunch of strangers and probably be rejected by most of them. But for a startup to succeed, at least one founder (usually the CEO) will have to spend a lot of time on sales and marketing.

The other reason founders ignore this path is that the absolute numbers seem so small at first. This can't be how the big, famous startups got started, they think. The mistake they make is to underestimate the power of compound growth. We encourage every startup to measure their progress by weekly growth rate. If you have 100 users, you need to get 10 more next week to grow 10% a week. And while 110 may not seem much better than 100, if you keep growing at 10% a week you'll be surprised how big the numbers get. After a year you'll have 14,000 users, and after 2 years you'll have 2 million.

sentences.original: One of the most common types of advice we give at Y Combinator is to do things that don't scale.
sentences.translation: Y Combinator에서 우리가 가장 흔히 하는 조언 중 하나는 확장 불가능한 일을 하라는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: One of the most common types of advice
grammarElements.label: 주어 (one of + 최상급)

grammarElements.text: to do things that don't scale
grammarElements.label: 보어 (to부정사구)

vocabulary.example: The business needs to scale quickly to survive.
She gave me some good advice on investing.

expressions.phrase: one of the most common types of
expressions.usage: 여러 가지 중 대표적인 것을 소개할 때
스타트업 세계에서 초기에 수작업으로 해야 하는 일을 지칭

patterns.pattern: One of the most [adjective] [noun] is to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 가장 대표적인 사례를 소개하는 에세이 오프닝 패턴. 핵심 주장을 첫 문장에 제시.
examples: One of the most important skills is to listen carefully.
One of the most effective strategies is to start small.

drillQuestions.question: One _____ the most common types of advice is to do things that don't scale.
The most common advice is to do things that don't _____.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 핵심 주장을 첫 문장에 바로 제시하는 Paul Graham 특유의 직접적 글쓰기 스타일. "things that don't scale"은 스타트업 업계에서 하나의 관용구가 된 표현이다. 관계대명사 that/which가 advice 뒤에서 생략되어 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: One of the most common types of advice
koreanAlignment.ko: 가장 흔한 조언 유형 중 하나는

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 핵심 명제 선언(thesis statement) — 에세이 전체의 핵심 주장을 첫 문장에 배치하여 독자의 관심을 즉시 잡음

sentences.original: A lot of would-be founders believe that startups either take off or don't.
sentences.translation: 많은 예비 창업자들은 스타트업이 성공하거나 실패하거나 둘 중 하나라고 믿습니다.

grammarElements.text: that startups either take off or don't
grammarElements.label: that절 (목적어)

vocabulary.example: The workshop attracted many would-be entrepreneurs.
The company really took off after the product launch.

expressions.usage: 두 가지 가능성만 존재한다고 말할 때
아직 창업하지 않았지만 창업을 원하는 사람을 지칭

patterns.pattern: A lot of [people] believe that [X] either [happens] or [doesn't]
patterns.explanation: 흔한 이분법적 오해를 소개하는 패턴. 뒤에서 이를 반박하기 위한 전제로 사용.
examples: A lot of students believe that you either have talent or you don't.
A lot of people believe that startups either succeed or fail.

drillQuestions.question: A lot of would-be founders _____ that startups either take off or don't.
Startups _____ take off or they don't.

sentences.notes: "would-be"는 하이픈으로 연결된 복합형용사로, "~이 되고자 하는"이라는 뜻. either...or 구조로 이분법적 사고를 표현한 뒤, 다음 문장에서 이를 반박하는 글쓰기 기법.
pronunciationNotes: either: /ˈiːðər/ 또는 /ˈaɪðər/ — 미국에서 두 발음 모두 사용

sentences.original: You build something, make it available, and if you've made a better mousetrap, people beat a path to your door as promised.
sentences.translation: 뭔가를 만들고, 사용 가능하게 하면, 더 좋은 쥐덫을 만들었다면 약속대로 사람들이 줄지어 찾아올 것이라고요.

grammarElements.text: if you've made a better mousetrap
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if + 현재완료)

grammarElements.text: people beat a path to your door
grammarElements.label: 주절 (관용구)

vocabulary.example: Build a better mousetrap and the world will beat a path to your door.

vocabulary.word: beat a path to one's door
vocabulary.example: Once the restaurant got a good review, customers beat a path to their door.
The product is now available online.

expressions.usage: 좋은 제품만 있으면 성공한다는 (잘못된) 믿음을 비유적으로 표현
기대한 대로 결과가 나타남을 의미. 여기서는 아이러니하게 사용됨

patterns.pattern: You [verb A], [verb B], and if [condition], [result]
patterns.explanation: 일련의 행동과 조건 결과를 나열하는 패턴. 누군가의 사고방식을 재현하듯 서술.
examples: You study hard, take the test, and if you've prepared well, you get a good score.
You practice daily, perform on stage, and if you've rehearsed enough, the audience loves it.

drillQuestions.question: If you've made a better mousetrap, people beat a _____ to your door.
You build something, make it _____, and wait for users.

sentences.notes: "Build a better mousetrap"은 에머슨(Emerson)에게서 유래된 유명한 미국 격언의 변형. 원문 각주에서 Paul Graham이 직접 에머슨의 실제 문구를 정정하고 있다. "beat a path to your door"는 수요가 자연스럽게 몰려든다는 뜻의 관용구.

koreanAlignment.en: if you've made a better mousetrap
people beat a path to your door

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 인유(allusion) — 에머슨의 유명한 격언을 인용하여 "좋은 제품이면 자동 성공"이라는 통념을 소개한 뒤 반박하려는 준비

sentences.original: Or they don't, in which case the market must not exist.
sentences.translation: 아니면 사람들이 오지 않고, 그 경우 시장이 존재하지 않는 것이 틀림없다고요.

vocabulary.example: The flight might be canceled, in which case we'll take the train.
If no one is buying, the demand must not exist.

expressions.usage: 앞의 상황이 발생했을 때 따르는 결론을 제시

patterns.pattern: Or [negative outcome], in which case [conclusion]
patterns.explanation: 대안적 시나리오와 그로부터의 결론을 연결하는 패턴. 잘못된 논리를 재현할 때 사용.
examples: Or the product fails, in which case the idea must be bad.
Or nobody responds, in which case the market must not exist.

drillQuestions.question: Or they don't, in _____ case the market must not exist.
The market must not _____.

sentences.notes: "in which case"는 격식체에서 "그 경우에"라는 의미로, 앞 절의 상황이 참일 때의 결론을 도입한다. "must not exist"는 강한 추측의 부정으로, 화자가 이 논리가 틀렸다고 생각하는 아이러니가 담겨 있다.
pronunciationNotes: in which case: 연음으로 "인 위치 케이스"처럼 발음, which의 /w/에 유의

sentences.original: Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off.
sentences.translation: 사실 스타트업이 성공하는 것은 창업자가 성공하게 만들기 때문입니다.

grammarElements.text: because the founders make them take off
grammarElements.label: 이유절 (because + 사역동사 make)

vocabulary.example: Actually, that's not quite right.
The team made the project take off with hard work.

expressions.usage: 주어가 목적어에게 행동을 하게 할 때. 사역동사 make + 목적어 + 동사원형

patterns.pattern: Actually, [X happens] because [someone makes it happen]
patterns.explanation: 앞서 제시한 통념을 "Actually"로 뒤집는 반박 패턴. 에세이에서 핵심 논점 전환.
examples: Actually, success happens because people work incredibly hard.
Actually, innovation occurs because teams push boundaries.

drillQuestions.question: Startups take off because the founders _____ them take off.
_____ startups take off because the founders make them take off.

sentences.notes: "Actually"로 시작하여 앞 문단의 통념(제품이 좋으면 자동 성공)을 단칼에 뒤집는다. "make them take off"에서 사역동사 make + 목적어 + 동사원형 구조가 핵심. 같은 구동사 take off를 반복하여 주체의 차이(자동 vs 능동)를 강조.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반박(refutation) — "Actually"로 시작하며 이전 단락의 통념을 정면으로 뒤집음

sentences.original: There may be a handful that just grew by themselves, but usually it takes some sort of push to get them going.
sentences.translation: 저절로 성장한 소수의 예외가 있을 수도 있지만, 보통은 시동을 걸기 위해 어떤 종류의 밀어주기가 필요합니다.

grammarElements.text: it takes some sort of push
grammarElements.label: 가주어 it + takes (필요하다)

vocabulary.example: Only a handful of people attended the meeting.
The project needed a big push to get started.
It took a while to get the project going.

expressions.usage: 정확하지 않지만 대략적인 분류를 할 때
외부 도움 없이 자체적으로 일어남을 표현

patterns.pattern: There may be [exceptions], but usually it takes [effort] to [achieve something]
patterns.explanation: 예외를 인정한 뒤 일반적 규칙을 강조하는 양보-대조 패턴.
examples: There may be a few natural talents, but usually it takes years of practice to master a skill.
There may be overnight successes, but usually it takes persistence to build a career.

drillQuestions.question: There may be a _____ that just grew by themselves.
It takes some sort of push to get them _____.

sentences.notes: "it takes + 목적어 + to부정사"는 "~하는 데 ~이 필요하다"라는 중요한 패턴. "get them going"은 "get + 목적어 + 현재분사"로 "~을 ~하게 하다"라는 사역의 의미.
pronunciationNotes: handful: /ˈhændfʊl/ — "핸드풀", d와 f 사이에서 d가 약하게 발음

sentences.original: A good metaphor would be the cranks that car engines had before they got electric starters.
sentences.translation: 좋은 비유는 자동차 엔진에 전기 시동장치가 생기기 전에 사용했던 크랭크일 것입니다.

vocabulary.example: The "melting pot" is a common metaphor for American society.
Old cars had to be started with a hand crank.
The electric starter made it easy to start a car.

expressions.usage: 추상적 개념을 구체적 비유로 설명할 때 도입부

patterns.pattern: A good metaphor would be [concrete example]
patterns.explanation: 추상적인 개념을 구체적 비유로 전환하는 패턴. 에세이에서 설명력을 높이는 기법.
examples: A good metaphor would be planting a seed and waiting for it to grow.
A good metaphor would be learning to ride a bicycle.

drillQuestions.question: A good metaphor _____ be the cranks that car engines had.
Car engines had cranks _____ they got electric starters.

sentences.notes: 자동차 크랭크 비유는 스타트업의 초기 수동 노력을 설명하는 핵심 이미지. 전기 시동 이전의 자동차처럼, 처음에는 힘든 수작업(크랭크 돌리기)이 필요하지만 일단 엔진이 돌아가면 자체 동력으로 움직인다는 의미. would be는 단정이 아닌 제안의 느낌.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 비유(analogy) — 크랭크와 전기 시동의 비유로 스타트업 초기의 수작업 필요성을 직관적으로 설명

sentences.original: Once the engine was going, it would keep going, but there was a separate and laborious process to get it going.
sentences.translation: 일단 엔진이 돌아가기 시작하면 계속 돌아가지만, 엔진을 돌리기 위한 별도의 힘든 과정이 있었습니다.

grammarElements.text: there was a separate and laborious process
grammarElements.label: There 구문

vocabulary.example: Data entry is a laborious task.
The building has a separate entrance for staff.

expressions.usage: 일단 시작된 것이 지속됨을 표현
무언가를 작동하게 만들 때

patterns.pattern: Once [X was happening], it would keep [happening], but there was [effort] to [start it]
patterns.explanation: 유지(keep going)와 시작(get going)의 차이를 대비하는 패턴. 관성의 힘 vs 초기 노력.
examples: Once the habit was formed, it would keep going, but there was effort needed to start.
Once the business was profitable, it would keep growing, but there was a long struggle to reach profitability.

drillQuestions.question: Once the engine was going, it would _____ going.
There was a separate and _____ process to get it going.

sentences.notes: "going"이 세 번 반복되면서 was going(상태) → keep going(지속) → get it going(시작)으로 의미가 미묘하게 변한다. 이 반복은 의도적이며, 독자에게 "시작"과 "유지"의 차이를 각인시킨다.

sentences.original: The most common unscalable thing founders have to do at the start is to recruit users manually.
sentences.translation: 창업자들이 초기에 해야 하는 가장 흔한 확장 불가능한 일은 사용자를 직접 모집하는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: founders have to do at the start
grammarElements.label: 관계절 (목적격 관계대명사 생략)

vocabulary.example: Manual customer support is unscalable beyond a certain point.
The company is recruiting new engineers.
The data was entered manually into the system.

expressions.usage: 시작 단계를 언급할 때

patterns.pattern: The most common [adjective] thing [people] have to do is to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 특정 그룹이 해야 하는 가장 보편적인 일을 소개하는 패턴.
examples: The most common mistake students have to avoid is to procrastinate.
The most important thing leaders have to do is to listen.

drillQuestions.question: The most common unscalable thing founders have to do is to _____ users manually.
Founders have to recruit users _____.

sentences.notes: "unscalable"은 일반 사전에 없는 조어(un- + scalable)로, 스타트업 문화에서 널리 사용된다. "recruit users manually"는 에세이 전체의 핵심 실천 메시지.

koreanAlignment.en: founders have to do at the start

sentences.original: Nearly all startups have to.
sentences.translation: 거의 모든 스타트업이 그래야 합니다.

vocabulary.example: Nearly everyone agreed with the decision.

expressions.usage: 예외가 극소수임을 강조할 때

patterns.pattern: Nearly all [group] have to.
patterns.explanation: 앞 문장의 동사를 생략하고 짧게 반복하여 보편성을 강조하는 패턴.
examples: Do you need to practice? Nearly all beginners have to.
Should we test early? Nearly all successful teams have to.

drillQuestions.question: _____ all startups have to recruit users manually.

sentences.notes: 앞 문장의 "recruit users manually"가 생략된 극도로 짧은 문장. 의도적인 간결함으로 메시지의 보편성과 불가피성을 강조한다. Paul Graham의 글쓰기 스타일에서 짧은 문장은 강조의 기능.

sentences.original: You can't wait for users to come to you.
sentences.translation: 사용자가 알아서 찾아오기를 기다릴 수 없습니다.

vocabulary.example: Don't wait for the perfect moment; start now.

expressions.usage: for + 목적어 + to부정사 구조로 기대되는 행동을 표현

patterns.pattern: You can't wait for [X] to [happen]. You have to [do Y].
patterns.explanation: 수동적 태도를 부정한 뒤 능동적 대안을 제시하는 패턴. 두 문장이 짝을 이룸.
examples: You can't wait for opportunities to come to you. You have to create them.
You can't wait for things to change. You have to make them change.

drillQuestions.question: You can't wait _____ users to come to you.

sentences.notes: 다음 문장과 짝을 이루는 대조 구문. "can't wait for X to come" vs "have to go out and get"의 수동-능동 대비가 핵심.

koreanAlignment.en: for users to come to you

sentences.original: You have to go out and get them.
sentences.translation: 직접 나가서 그들을 데려와야 합니다.

vocabulary.example: You need to go out and meet new people.

expressions.usage: 적극적으로 행동하여 원하는 것을 얻을 때

patterns.pattern: You have to go out and [verb] them.
patterns.explanation: 능동적 행동을 촉구하는 직접적이고 간결한 명령 패턴.
examples: You have to go out and find customers.
You have to go out and build relationships.

drillQuestions.question: You have to go _____ and get them.

sentences.notes: 극도로 짧고 직접적인 문장. 앞 문장의 "can't wait"과 대비되어 행동의 필요성을 강조. "go out and get"은 구어적이고 행동 지향적인 표현으로, Paul Graham의 실용적 조언 스타일을 잘 보여준다.
pronunciationNotes: go out and get: 연음으로 "고우라웃 앤 겟뎀"처럼 발음. and는 약하게 /ən/으로

sentences.original: Stripe is one of the most successful startups we've funded, and the problem they solved was an urgent one.
sentences.translation: Stripe는 우리가 투자한 가장 성공적인 스타트업 중 하나이고, 그들이 해결한 문제는 긴급한 문제였습니다.

grammarElements.text: one of the most successful startups we've funded
grammarElements.label: 보어 (관계절 포함)

vocabulary.example: The project was funded by venture capitalists.
There is an urgent need for clean water.

expressions.usage: 최상급을 사용하되 유일하지 않음을 표현

patterns.pattern: [X] is one of the most [adjective] [noun] we've [past participle]
patterns.explanation: 개인적 경험에 기반한 평가를 전달하는 패턴. we've로 1인칭 경험의 신뢰성을 부여.
examples: She is one of the most talented engineers we've hired.
This is one of the most challenging projects we've undertaken.

drillQuestions.question: Stripe is one of the most successful startups we've _____.
The problem they solved was an _____ one.

sentences.notes: "we've funded"는 Y Combinator의 입장에서의 발언. "an urgent one"에서 one은 대명사로 problem을 대체. Stripe(결제 서비스)를 구체적 사례로 들어 주장의 설득력을 높인다.

koreanAlignment.en: Stripe is one of the most successful startups
koreanAlignment.ko: Stripe는 가장 성공적인 스타트업 중 하나이고

sentences.original: If anyone could have sat back and waited for users, it was Stripe.
sentences.translation: 만약 누군가가 편히 앉아서 사용자를 기다릴 수 있었다면, 그건 Stripe였을 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: If anyone could have sat back and waited for users
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (가정법 과거완료)

vocabulary.example: You can't just sit back and hope things will improve.

expressions.phrase: if anyone could have ... it was [X]
expressions.meaning: 만약 누군가가 ~할 수 있었다면, 그건 X였을 것이다
expressions.usage: 가장 자격이 있는 대상조차도 그렇게 하지 않았음을 강조
소극적 태도를 비유적으로 표현

patterns.pattern: If anyone could have [past participle], it was [X].
patterns.explanation: 가장 유리한 위치에 있던 X조차 그렇게 하지 않았다는 것을 암시하는 양보 패턴.
examples: If anyone could have skipped the hard work, it was Einstein.
If anyone could have relied on reputation alone, it was Apple.

drillQuestions.question: If anyone could have sat _____ and waited, it was Stripe.
If anyone _____ have waited for users, it was Stripe.

sentences.notes: 가정법 과거완료(could have sat back)를 사용한 수사적 문장. "만약 기다려도 됐던 회사가 있다면 Stripe였는데, 그들조차 기다리지 않았다"는 의미. 뒤이어 나오는 "But in fact"와 함께 논증의 핵심 사례를 구성.

koreanAlignment.en: If anyone could have sat back
koreanAlignment.ko: 만약 누군가가 편히 앉아 있을 수 있었다면

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 양보(concession) — 가장 유리한 위치의 사례를 인정한 뒤, 그조차 수동적이지 않았음을 보여줌

sentences.original: But in fact they're famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition.
sentences.translation: 하지만 사실 그들은 YC 내에서 공격적인 초기 사용자 확보로 유명합니다.

vocabulary.example: The company pursued an aggressive marketing strategy.
User acquisition is the key challenge for new apps.

expressions.usage: 기대와 반대되는 사실을 도입할 때
특정 특성이나 업적으로 알려져 있음을 표현

patterns.pattern: But in fact [they]'re famous for [opposite of expectation]
patterns.explanation: 앞서 설정한 기대를 반전시키는 패턴. "사실은 정반대다"라는 극적 효과.
examples: But in fact they're famous for working harder than anyone.
But in fact she's known for being the most prepared person in the room.

drillQuestions.question: They're famous _____ YC for aggressive early user acquisition.
They're famous for aggressive early user _____.

sentences.notes: "But in fact"는 이전 문장의 가정법(could have waited)을 뒤집는 반전의 연결어. "aggressive"는 비즈니스 맥락에서 부정적이 아닌 "적극적인, 강력한"이라는 긍정적 의미로 사용.

sentences.original: At YC we use the term "Collison installation" for the technique they invented.
sentences.translation: YC에서 우리는 그들이 발명한 기법을 "콜리슨 설치"라고 부릅니다.

vocabulary.example: The term "unicorn" refers to a billion-dollar startup.
The installation of the software was quick.
She learned a new painting technique.

expressions.usage: 특정 현상이나 기법에 고유한 이름을 붙여 설명할 때

patterns.pattern: We use the term "[name]" for [something]
patterns.explanation: 새로운 용어나 내부 은어를 소개하는 패턴. 독자에게 전문 지식을 공유하는 효과.
examples: We use the term "pivot" for changing business direction.
Scientists use the term "neuroplasticity" for the brain's ability to change.

drillQuestions.question: At YC we use the _____ "Collison installation" for the technique.
We use the term for the technique they _____.

sentences.notes: "Collison installation"은 Stripe 공동 창업자 Patrick과 John Collison의 성에서 유래. YC 내부에서 쓰이는 은어(jargon)를 소개함으로써, Paul Graham이 내부자 시점에서 이야기하고 있음을 보여준다.

sentences.original: But the Collison brothers weren't going to wait.
sentences.translation: 하지만 콜리슨 형제는 기다릴 생각이 없었습니다.

vocabulary.example: The Wright brothers invented the airplane.

expressions.usage: 의지적으로 수동적 태도를 거부함을 표현

patterns.pattern: But [someone] wasn't going to [passive action].
patterns.explanation: 대조를 통해 주체의 강한 행동 의지를 강조하는 패턴.
examples: But she wasn't going to give up.
But the team wasn't going to accept defeat.

drillQuestions.question: The Collison brothers _____ going to wait.

sentences.notes: 짧고 강렬한 전환 문장. "weren't going to"는 미래에 대한 의지/계획의 부정으로, 단순 과거(didn't wait)보다 더 의도적이고 결연한 뉘앙스를 준다.

sentences.original: When anyone agreed to try Stripe they'd say "Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot.
sentences.translation: 누군가 Stripe를 사용해보겠다고 동의하면 "좋습니다, 노트북 주세요"라고 말하고 그 자리에서 바로 설정해 주었습니다.

grammarElements.text: When anyone agreed to try Stripe
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — when)

vocabulary.example: She was hired on the spot after the interview.
Can you help me set up my new phone?
He agreed to help with the project.

expressions.usage: 동의를 받은 즉시 행동으로 옮길 때 사용하는 구어체
지체 없이 즉시 행동함을 강조

patterns.pattern: When [someone agreed], they'd [immediately act] on the spot.
patterns.explanation: 동의를 받는 즉시 행동으로 전환하는 과감한 실행력을 묘사하는 패턴.
examples: When a customer showed interest, they'd demo the product on the spot.
When someone expressed a need, they'd build a solution on the spot.

drillQuestions.question: They'd say "give me your laptop" and set them up on the _____.
When anyone agreed to _____ Stripe, they'd set them up immediately.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 "Collison installation"의 구체적 모습을 보여주는 핵심 일화. "Right then, give me your laptop"이라는 직접 인용이 생생한 현장감을 준다. "they'd"는 would의 축약으로 과거의 반복적 행동을 나타낸다.

koreanAlignment.en: When anyone agreed to try Stripe
and set them up on the spot

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 일화(anecdote) — 직접 인용과 구체적 행동 묘사로 "Collison installation"의 실체를 생생하게 전달

sentences.original: There are two reasons founders resist going out and recruiting users individually.
sentences.translation: 창업자들이 직접 나가서 사용자를 일일이 모집하는 것을 꺼리는 이유는 두 가지입니다.

grammarElements.text: going out and recruiting users individually
grammarElements.label: 동명사구 (resist의 목적어)

vocabulary.example: Many people resist change.
Each student was assessed individually.

expressions.usage: resist 뒤에 동명사(-ing)가 오는 패턴

patterns.pattern: There are [number] reasons [people] resist [doing something]
patterns.explanation: 저항/꺼림의 이유를 구조적으로 열거하기 위한 도입 패턴.
examples: There are three reasons students resist public speaking.
There are two reasons companies resist remote work.

drillQuestions.question: There are two reasons founders _____ going out and recruiting users.
Founders resist recruiting users _____.

sentences.notes: "resist + 동명사"는 중요한 문법 포인트. resist 뒤에는 반드시 동명사(-ing)가 온다(to부정사 불가). 이 문장은 새로운 논점(왜 창업자들이 이것을 꺼리는가)으로의 전환을 알린다.

koreanAlignment.en: going out and recruiting users individually
koreanAlignment.ko: 직접 나가서 사용자를 일일이 모집하는 것을

sentences.original: They'd rather sit at home writing code than go out and talk to a bunch of strangers and probably be rejected by most of them.
sentences.translation: 그들은 밖에 나가서 모르는 사람들에게 말을 걸고 대부분에게 거절당하느니, 차라리 집에 앉아서 코드를 쓰는 것을 선호합니다.

grammarElements.text: 'd rather sit at home writing code
grammarElements.label: 동사 (would rather — 선호)

grammarElements.text: than go out and talk to a bunch of strangers
grammarElements.label: 비교절 (than)

grammarElements.text: and probably be rejected by most of them
grammarElements.label: 병렬절 (수동태)

vocabulary.example: I'd rather stay home than go to the party.
I met a bunch of interesting people at the conference.
The proposal was rejected by the committee.
Don't talk to strangers, my mother always said.

expressions.usage: 두 선택지 중 하나를 선호함을 표현. would rather 뒤에는 동사원형
수동태로 거절의 경험을 표현

patterns.pattern: They'd rather [comfortable action] than [uncomfortable but necessary action]
patterns.explanation: 편한 것을 택하고 불편하지만 필요한 것을 피하는 인간 심리를 묘사하는 패턴.
examples: Students would rather review notes than take practice tests.
People would rather scroll social media than exercise.

drillQuestions.question: They'd _____ sit at home writing code than go out and talk to strangers.
They would probably be _____ by most of them.

sentences.notes: "would rather A than B"는 선호를 표현하는 핵심 패턴으로, 뒤에 동사원형이 온다. 이 긴 문장은 "sit at home writing code"(편안함)와 "go out and talk to strangers and be rejected"(불편함)의 대비를 통해 창업자의 심리를 생생하게 묘사한다. "writing code"는 현재분사의 부대상황 용법.

koreanAlignment.en: than go out and talk to
and probably be rejected by most of them

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 공감적 묘사(empathetic description) — 창업자의 내면적 꺼림을 솔직하게 묘사하여 독자의 공감을 이끌어냄
